title: EXPOSÉ V CONSTRUCTION OF QUOTIENT SCHEMES

pages.title: EXPOSÉ V CONSTRUCTION OF QUOTIENT SCHEMES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ V
CONSTRUCTION OF QUOTIENT SCHEMESby P. Gabriel

The purpose of this exposé is to prove the theorems stated in TDTE III. If X and T are two objects of a category \(\mathscr{C}\) we write \(X(T)\) instead of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}}(T,X)\). Similarly, if \(\varphi : Y \to X\) and \(f : U \to T\) are two arrows of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\varphi(T)\) denotes the map \(g \mapsto \varphi \circ g\) from \(Y(T)\) to \(X(T)\), and \(Y(f)\) the map \(g \mapsto g \circ f\) from \(Y(T)\) to \(Y(U)\). Finally, if P is a scheme, we denote by \(\underline{P}\) the set underlying P.

Exceptionally, in the present exposé we do not follow the convention stated in IV 4.6.15 concerning the notation for quotients (loc. cit. top of page 68), because here we wish to give a construction of quotients that applies equally to "equivalence prerelations" which are not equivalence relations.
1. \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoids

\(\mathscr{C}\) is a category in which products and fibered products exist. Let us first recall that a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_0} & X_0 \ar[r]^{p} & Y } \] in \(\mathscr{C}\) is said to be exact if \(pd_0 = pd_1\) and if, for every \(T \in \mathscr{C}\), \(T(p)\) is a bijection from \(T(Y)\) onto the subset of \(T(X_0)\) consisting of the arrows \(f : X_0 \to T\) such that \(fd_0 = fd_1\). We also say that \((Y,p)\) is the cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) and we write \[ (Y,p) = \mathrm{Coker}(d_0,d_1). \]

Let for example \(\mathscr{C}\) be the category of ringed spaces. In this case, a cokernel \((Y,p)\) always exists, and it admits the following description: the space underlying Y is obtained from \(X_0\) by identifying the points \(d_0(x)\) and \(d_1(x)\) and endowing Y with the quotient topology. The canonical map \(\pi : X \to Y\) and \(d_0, d_1\) then induce a double arrow of sheaves of rings on Y \[ \xymatrix{ \pi_*(\mathscr{O}_0) \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{\delta_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{\delta_1} & \pi_*(d_{0*}\mathscr{O}_1) = \pi_*(d_{1*}\mathscr{O}_1) } \]

where \(\mathscr{O}_i\) is the structure sheaf of \(X_i\). As the sheaf of rings on Y one takes the subsheaf of \(\pi_*(\mathscr{O}_0)\) whose sections s are such that \(\delta_0(s) = \delta_1(s)\). The arrow p is defined in the obvious way. In this exposé, we study the existence of \(\mathrm{Coker}(d_0,d_1)\) when the double arrow \((d_0,d_1)\) is embedded in a richer context; more precisely, denote by \(X_2 = X_1 \times_{d_1,d_0} X_1\) the fibered product of the diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ & X_1 \ar[d]^{d_1} \\ X_1 \ar[r]_{d_0} & X_0 } \]
and by \(d_0'\) and \(d_2'\) the two canonical projections of \(X_2\) onto \(X_1\); by definition we thus have a cartesian square

Moreover, let us give ourselves a third arrow \(d_1' : X_2 \to X_1\); we say that \((d_0, d_1 : X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0, d_1')\) is a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid if for every object T of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(X_1(T)\) is the set of arrows of a groupoid \(X_*(T)\) whose set of objects is \(X_0(T)\), whose source map is \(d_1(T)\), whose target map is \(d_0(T)\), and whose composition map is \(d_1'(T)\) (as usual we identify \((X_1 \times_{d_1,d_0} X_1)(T)\) with \(X_1(T) \times_{d_1(T),d_0(T)} X_1(T)\); recall also that a groupoid is a category all of whose arrows are invertible). If \(\varphi\) is an arrow of the groupoid \(X_*(T)\), the map \(f \mapsto \varphi \circ f\) is a bijection from the set of arrows f whose target coincides with the source of \(\varphi\) onto the set of arrows having the same target as \(\varphi\). One easily sees that this fact can be expressed by saying that the square
is cartesian.

Similarly, the map \(g \mapsto g \circ \varphi\) is a bijection from the set of arrows g of \(X_*(T)\) whose source is the target of \(\varphi\) onto the set of arrows having the same source as \(\varphi\). This fact can again be expressed by saying that the square
is cartesian.

On the other hand, let us denote by \(s : X_0 \to X_1\) the unique arrow of \(\mathscr{C}\) such that \(s(T) : X_0(T) \to X_1(T)\) assigns to every object of \(X_*(T)\) the identity arrow of that object. The arrow s satisfies the equalities \[ d_1 s = \mathrm{id}_{X_0}, \qquad (3) \qquad\qquad\qquad d_0 s = \mathrm{id}_{X_0}. \qquad (3\text{ bis}) \] Finally, the associativity of the composition maps \(d_1'(T)\) is expressed by the commutativity of the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ X_1\times_{d_1,d_0} X_1 \times_{d_1,d_0} X_1 \ar[rr]^-{d_1' \times X_1} \ar[d]_-{X_1 \times d_1'} & & X_1 \times_{d_1,d_0} X_1 \ar[d]^-{d_1'} \\ X_1 \times_{d_1,d_0} X_1 \ar[rr]_-{d_1'} & & X_1 } \] Conversely, conditions (1), (2) and (4) and the existence of an arrow s satisfying (3) imply that \((X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0, d_1')\) is a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid. Condition (3) is harmless; it simply ensures that the map \(d_1(T) : X_1(T) \to X_0(T)\) is surjective for every \(T \in \mathscr{C}\). In the rest of this exposé, we mainly use the cartesian squares (0), (1) and (2), which we summarize in the diagram

In this diagram the two left-hand squares (i.e. squares (0) and (2)) are cartesian; the first row is exact and \(X_2\) is identified with the fibered product \(X_1 \times_{d_0,d_0} X_1\). We use associativity only in an indirect way, for instance to guarantee the existence of an arrow s satisfying (3) and (3 bis), or to guarantee the existence of an arrow \(\sigma : X_1 \to X_1\) such that \(d_0\sigma = d_1\) and \(d_1\sigma = d_0\) (one chooses \(\sigma\) in such a way that \(\sigma(T) : X_1(T) \to X_1(T)\) sends every arrow of \(X_*(T)\) to its inverse arrow).

By abuse of language we shall sometimes call a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{d_0'} \ar[r]|-{d_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] such that (0), (1) and (2) are cartesian, (4) is commutative, and there exists s satisfying (3). The object \(X_2\) may thus be "a" fibered product of (*) without being "the" fibered product of (*). Instead of the \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid \(X_*\), we shall also speak of the groupoid \(X_*\) with base \(X_0\), or of the equivalence prerelation \(X_*\) in \(X_0\).
2. Examples of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoids

a) Let X be an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) and G a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group acting on X on the left. We denote by \(d_0 : G\times X \to X\) the arrow defining the action of G on X, by \(d_1 : G\times X \to X\) the projection of the product onto the second factor, by \(\mu : G\times G \to G\) the arrow defining the \(\mathscr{C}\)-group structure of G, and finally by \(\mathrm{pr}_{2,3}\) the projection of \(G\times G\times X = G\times(G\times X)\) onto the second factor. Then \[ \xymatrix{ G\times G\times X \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{\mathrm{pr}_{2,3}} \ar[r]|-{\mu\times X} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{G\times d_0} & G\times X \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_0} & X } \] is a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid.

b) Let \(d_0, d_1 : X_1 \to X_0\) be an equivalence pair. If \(d_0 \boxtimes d_1 : X_1 \to X_0\times X_0\) is the arrow with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\), we thus assume that \((d_0\boxtimes d_1)(T)\) is, for every object T of \(\mathscr{C}\), a bijection from \(X_1(T)\) onto the graph of an equivalence relation on \(X_0(T)\). The set \(X_1(T)\) is consequently identified with the set of pairs \((x,y)\) of elements of \(X_0(T)\) such that \(x \sim y\); similarly, the set \(X_2(T) = (X_1\times_{d_1,d_0} X_1)(T)\) is identified with the set of triples \((x,y,z)\) of elements of \(X_0(T)\) such that \(x \sim y\) and \(y \sim z\). There is therefore one and only one arrow \(d_1' : X_2 \to X_1\) making the squares (1) and (2) commutative: \(d_1'(T)\) must send \((x,y,z) \in X_2(T)\) to \((x,z) \in X_1(T)\). For this choice of \(d_1'\), \((d_0, d_1 : X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0, d_1')\) is a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid.

Conversely, consider a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid \(X_*\) such that \(d_0 \boxtimes d_1 : X_1 \to X_0\times X_0\) is a monomorphism. Then \((d_0,d_1)\) is an equivalence pair and \(X_*\) can be reconstructed from \((d_0,d_1)\) as explained a few lines above.

c) If \(p : X \to Y\) is an arbitrary arrow of \(\mathscr{C}\) and if \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) are the two projections of \(X\times_{p,p} X\) onto X, then \((\mathrm{pr}_1,\mathrm{pr}_2) : X\times_{p,p} X \rightrightarrows X\) is an equivalence pair. We say that p is an effective epimorphism if the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X\times_{p,p} X \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{\mathrm{pr}_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{\mathrm{pr}_2} & X \ar[r]^{p} & Y } \] is exact, that is, if \((Y,p) = \mathrm{Coker}(\mathrm{pr}_1,\mathrm{pr}_2)\).

Let for example S be a noetherian scheme and let \(\mathscr{C}\) be the category of schemes finite over S. Let us show that an epimorphism of \(\mathscr{C}\) is not necessarily effective: we choose S equal to \(\mathrm{Spec}\ k[T^3,T^5]\), where k is a commutative field, Y equal to S and X equal to \(\mathrm{Spec}\ k[T]\). If i is the inclusion of \(B = k[T^3,T^5]\) into \(A = k[T]\), p is chosen equal to \(\mathrm{Spec}\ i\). In this case \(X\times_{p,p} X\) is identified with \(\mathrm{Spec}(A\otimes_B A)\) and \(\mathrm{Coker}(\mathrm{pr}_1,\mathrm{pr}_2)\) with \(\mathrm{Spec}\ B'\), where \(B'\) is the subring of A consisting of the elements a such that \(a\otimes_B 1 = 1\otimes_B a\). Now \[ T^7\otimes_B 1 = (T^2T^5)\otimes_B 1 = T^2\otimes_B T^5 = T^2\otimes_B(T^3T^2) = T^5\otimes_B T^2 = 1\otimes_B T^7. \] Hence \(T^7\) belongs to \(B'\) but does not belong to B, and \(\mathrm{Spec}\ B'\) is distinct from \(\mathrm{Spec}\ B\), whence the counterexample.
3. Some sorites on \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoids
Here, in no particular order, are some remarks used in what follows:

a) Let \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{d_0'} \ar[r]|-{d_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] be a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid and \(f_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\) an arrow of \(\mathscr{C}\). We are going to define a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid with base \(Y_0\) \[ \xymatrix{ Y_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{e_0'} \ar[r]|-{e_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{e_2'} & Y_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{e_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{e_1} & Y_0 } \] which will be said to be induced by \(X_*\) and \(f_0\). We shall also say that \(Y_*\) is the inverse image of \(X_*\) under the base change \(f_0\).

We choose for \(Y_1\) the fibered product of the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ Y_1 \ar@{-->}[r]^-{f_1} \ar@{-->}[d] & X_1 \ar[d]^{d_0\boxtimes d_1} \\ Y_0\times Y_0 \ar[r]_-{f_0\times f_0} & X_0\times X_0 } \] and for \(e_0\) and \(e_1\) the arrows obtained by composing the canonical arrow \(Y_1 \to Y_0\times Y_0\) with the first and second projections of \(Y_0\times Y_0\). One may say that the pair \((e_0,e_1)\) is defined in such a way that, for every \(T \in \mathscr{C}\) and for every pair \((y,x)\) of elements of \(Y_0(T)\), there is a certain one-to-one correspondence \(\psi \mapsto {}_y\psi_x\) between the arrows \(\psi\) of \(X_*(T)\) with source \(f_0(x)\) and target \(f_0(y)\) and the arrows \({}_y\psi_x\) of \(Y_*(T)\) with source x and target y. One then determines \(e_1' : Y_2 \to Y_1\) by defining for every \(T \in \mathscr{C}\) the composition of arrows of \(Y_*(T)\) by means of the formula \[ {}_z\psi_y \circ {}_y\varphi_x = {}_z(\psi\circ\varphi)_x. \] It is clear that this definition makes each \(Y_*(T)\) a groupoid.

b) Knowing the \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid \(X_*\) and the base change \(f_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\), one can reconstruct the pair \((e_0,e_1) : Y_1 \rightrightarrows Y_0\) in another way: construct \(Y_0\times_{X_0} X_1\), \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) in such a way that the square \[ \xymatrix{ Y_0\times_{X_0} X_1 \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{pr}_1} & X_1 \ar[d]^{d_0} \\ Y_0 \ar[r]_-{f_0} & X_0 } \] is cartesian. One then verifies without difficulty, by reduction to the set-theoretic case, that one can define \(Y_1\) by means of the cartesian square: \[ \xymatrix{ Y_1 \ar[r]^-{e_0\boxtimes f_1} \ar[d]_-{e_1} & Y_0\times_{X_0} X_1 \ar[d]^{d_1\circ\mathrm{pr}_2} \\ Y_0 \ar[r]_-{f_0} & X_0 } \] where \(f_1\) denotes the canonical projection of \(Y_1 = (Y_0\times Y_0)\times_{(X_0\times X_0)} X_1\) onto \(X_1\).

c) We shall give an example of the inverse image of a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid: take \(Y_0\) equal to \(X_1\) and \(f_0\) equal to \(d_0\). For every object T of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(Y_1(T)\) is then identified with the set of diagrams of the form \[ \xymatrix{ b \ar[r]^{\varphi} & d \\ a \ar[u]^{f} & c \ar[u]_{g} } \] in \(X_*(T)\). The source of such a diagram is the arrow f, the target is the arrow g. These diagrams compose in the obvious way.

Now set \(Y_0' = X_1\), \(f_0' = d_1\) (we add the primes to avoid any confusion with the preceding example). In this case, \(Y_1'(T)\) is identified for every \(T\in\mathscr{C}\) with the set of diagrams of the form \[ \xymatrix{ b & & d \\ a \ar[u]^{f} \ar[rr]_-{\psi} & & c \ar[u]_{g} } \] of the groupoid \(X_*(T)\). The source of such a diagram is f, the target is g; the composition of these diagrams is obvious.

This being said, it is clear that the identity map of \(Y_0(T)\) and the map \[ \xymatrix{ b \ar[r]^{\varphi} & d \\ a \ar[u]^{f} & c \ar[u]_{g} } \qquad\longmapsto\qquad \xymatrix{ b & d \\ a \ar[u]^{f} \ar[r]_{g^{-1}\varphi f} & c \ar[u]_{g} } \] from \(Y_1(T)\) to \(Y_1'(T)\) define an isomorphism of the groupoid \(Y_*(T)\) onto \(Y_*'(T)\). Moreover, this isomorphism depends functorially on T, so that the \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoids \(Y_*\) and \(Y_*'\) are isomorphic.
d)

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1

We keep the notation of a) and we assume that \(f_0\) is a universal effective epimorphism. In this case, \(\mathrm{Coker}(d_0,d_1)\) exists if and only if \(\mathrm{Coker}(e_0,e_1)\) exists. The equality \((X,p) = \mathrm{Coker}(d_0,d_1)\) implies \((X,pf_0) = \mathrm{Coker}(e_0,e_1)\).

Let us first recall that an epimorphism \(f_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\) is said to be universal if, for every cartesian square \[ \xymatrix{ Y' \ar[r] \ar[d]_{f'} & Y_0 \ar[d]^{f_0} \\ X' \ar[r] & X_0 } \] \(f'\) is an epimorphism. This being so, denote by \(\mathrm{C}(d_0,d_1)\) the covariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) to sets which assigns to every \(T\in\mathscr{C}\) the kernel of the pair \(T(d_0), T(d_1) : T(X_0) \rightrightarrows T(X_1)\). Define \(\mathrm{C}(e_0,e_1)\) similarly. For every \(T \in \mathscr{C}\), we thus have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{C}(d_0,d_1)(T) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\mathrm{T}(f)} & T(X_0) \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{\mathrm{T}(d_1)} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{\mathrm{T}(d_0)} \ar[d]_{\mathrm{T}(f_0)} & T(X_1) \ar[d]^{\mathrm{T}(f_1)} \\ \mathrm{C}(e_0,e_1)(T) \ar[r] & T(Y_0) \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{\mathrm{T}(e_1)} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{\mathrm{T}(e_0)} & T(Y_1) } \] where \(\mathrm{T}(f)\) is the injection induced by the injection \(\mathrm{T}(f_0)\). If we show that \(\mathrm{T}(f)\) is a surjection for every T, we will have a functorial isomorphism \(f : \mathrm{C}(d_0,d_1) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{C}(e_0,e_1)\), so that the representability of one of these functors will be equivalent to that of the other; this will prove our proposition.

To prove the surjectivity of \(\mathrm{T}(f)\), consider the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & & Y_1 \ar[rr]^{f_1} \ar@<0.6ex>[d]^{e_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[d]_{e_1} & & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[d]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[d]_{d_1} \\ Y_0\times_{X_0} Y_0 \ar@<0.6ex>[rr]^{\mathrm{pr}_2} \ar@<-0.6ex>[rr]_{\mathrm{pr}_1} \ar[urr]^{\Delta} & & Y_0 \ar[rr]_{f_0} & & X_0 } \] where \(\Delta\) is defined by the equalities \((e_0\boxtimes e_1)\Delta = \mathrm{pr}_1\boxtimes\mathrm{pr}_2\) and \(f_1\Delta = sf_0\mathrm{pr}_1\), the arrow \(s : X_0 \to X_1\) satisfying equalities (3) and (3 bis) of Section 1.

If the arrow \(g : Y_0 \to T\) is such that \(g\circ e_0 = g\circ e_1\), we have \(g\circ e_0\circ\Delta = g\circ e_1\circ\Delta\), hence \(g\circ\mathrm{pr}_1 = g\circ\mathrm{pr}_2\). Since \(f_0\) is an effective epimorphism, g is the composite of \(f_0\) and an arrow \(h : X_0 \to T\), that is, we have \(g = \mathrm{T}(f_0)(h)\). It remains to show that h belongs to \(\mathrm{C}(d_0,d_1)(T)\), that is, satisfies the equality \(hd_0 = hd_1\); now we have \[ hd_0f_1 = hf_0e_0 = ge_0 = ge_1 = hf_0e_1 = hd_1f_1, \] whence the desired equality, thanks to the fact that \(f_1\) is an epimorphism (because \(f_0\) is a universal epimorphism).

e) Now consider a scheme S and choose \(\mathscr{C}\) equal to \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\). The datum of a \(\mathscr{C}\)-groupoid \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{d_0'} \ar[r]|-{d_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] allows us to define an equivalence relation on the set \(\underline{X_0}\) underlying the scheme \(X_0\): if \(x,y \in \underline{X_0}\), we shall write \(x\sim y\) when there exists \(z\in\underline{X_1}\) such that \(x = d_1 z\) and \(y = d_0 z\). The reflexivity and symmetry of this relation are obvious; let us prove transitivity: if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\), there exist \(u,v\in\underline{X_1}\) such that \(x = d_1u\), \(y = d_0u\), \(y = d_1v\), \(z = d_0v\). It follows that \((v,u)\) belongs to the set-theoretic fibered product \(\underline{X_1}\times_{d_1,d_0}\underline{X_1}\). Since the canonical map \[ \underline{X_1\times_{d_1,d_0} X_1} \longrightarrow \underline{X_1}\times_{d_1,d_0}\underline{X_1} \] from the set underlying the fibered product to the fibered product of the underlying sets is surjective, \((v,u)\) is the image of some \(w\in\underline{X_2}\). We then have \(x = d_1d_1'w\) and \(z = d_0d_1'w\), whence \(x\sim z\).

f) Let us keep the notation of a) and b), \(\mathscr{C}\) still being equal to \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\). If \(x,y\) are points of \(\underline{Y_0}\), we shall see that \(x\sim y\) if and only if \(f_0(x)\sim f_0(y)\) (the inverse image of the equivalence relation defined by a groupoid is the equivalence relation defined by the inverse image of the groupoid).

Indeed, suppose that \(x\sim y\). There then exists \(z\in\underline{Y_1}\) such that \(x = e_1z\), \(y = e_0z\). We then have \(f_0(x) = d_1f_1z\) and \(f_0(y) = d_0f_1z\), whence \(f_0(x)\sim f_0(y)\).

Conversely, suppose that \(f_0(x)\sim f_0(y)\) and let \(z\in\underline{X_1}\) be such that \(f_0(y) = d_1z\), \(f_0(x) = d_0z\). There is then a point t of \(\underline{Y_0\times_{X_0} X_1}\) such that \(\mathrm{pr}_1(t) = x\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2(t) = z\) (the notation is that of b)). Similarly, since \(f_0(y) = d_1\mathrm{pr}_2(t)\), there is an \(s\in\underline{Y_1}\) such that \(y = e_1(s)\) and \((e_0\boxtimes f_1)(s) = t\). We then have \(e_0(s) = \mathrm{pr}_1(e_0\boxtimes f_1)(s) = \mathrm{pr}_1(t) = x\). Whence \(x\sim y\).

pages.title: 4. Passage to the quotient by a finite flat equivalence prerelation(proof of a special case)

blocks.html:
4. Passage to the quotient by a finite flat equivalence prerelation
(proof of a special case)

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1

Let \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{d_0'} \ar[r]|-{d_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] be a \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid such that \(d_1\) is finite locally free and such that, for every \(x\in X_0\), the set \(d_0d_1^{-1}(x)\) is contained in an affine open subset of \(X_0\). Then:

  1. (i)

    There exists a cokernel \((Y,p)\) of \((d_0,d_1)\) in \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\); moreover, such a \((Y,p)\) is a cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) in the category of all ringed spaces.

  2. (ii)

    p is integral, and Y is affine if \(X_0\) is affine.

  3. (iii)

    The morphism \(X_1 \to X_0\times_Y X_0\) with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) is surjective.

  4. (iv)

    If \((d_0,d_1)\) is an equivalence pair, \(X_1 \to X_0\times_Y X_0\) is an isomorphism and \(p : X_0 \to Y\) is finite locally free.

It clearly follows from (i) that the topological space underlying Y is the quotient of the topological space underlying \(X_0\) by the equivalence relation defined by the \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid \(X_*\).

We shall first prove this theorem when \(X_0\) is affine and \(d_1\) is locally free of constant rank n. We shall then see how one can reduce to this special case: In the case we have placed ourselves in, \(X_0\), \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are affine. We may therefore assume that we have \[ X_i = \mathrm{Spec}\ A_i \ , \qquad d_j = \mathrm{Spec}\ \delta_j \ , \qquad d_k' = \mathrm{Spec}\ \delta_k', \] where the \(A_i\) are commutative rings and the \(\delta_j\), \(\delta_k'\) are ring homomorphisms. One may then replace the diagram (0,1,2) by the following one

\[ \xymatrix{ A_2 & A_1 \ar@<1.1ex>[l]_{\delta_0'} \ar[l]|-{\delta_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[l]^{\delta_2'} \\ A_1 \ar[u] & A_0 \ar@<0.6ex>[l]_{\delta_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[l]^{\delta_1} \ar[u] } \] in which the two left-hand squares are cocartesian.
Denote by B the subring of \(A_0\) consisting of the \(a\in A_0\) such that \(\delta_0(a) = \delta_1(a)\).
a) \(A_0\) is integral over B:

If a belongs to \(A_0\), let \[ \mathrm{P}_{\delta_1}(\mathrm{T},\delta_0(a)) = \mathrm{T}^n - \sigma_1\mathrm{T}^{n-1} + \cdots + (-1)^n\sigma_n \] be the characteristic polynomial of \(\delta_0(a)\) when \(A_1\) is regarded as an algebra over \(A_0\) by means of the homomorphism \(\delta_1\) (cf. Bourbaki, Alg. VIII, § 12 and Alg. comm. II, § 5, exercise 9). Since the left-hand squares of (0,1,2)* are cocartesian, we have \[ \delta_0(\mathrm{P}_{\delta_1}(\mathrm{T},\delta_0(a))) = \mathrm{P}_{\delta_2'}(\mathrm{T},\delta_0'\delta_0(a)) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \delta_1(\mathrm{P}_{\delta_1}(\mathrm{T},\delta_0(a))) = \mathrm{P}_{\delta_2'}(\mathrm{T},\delta_1'\delta_0(a)). \] Since \(\delta_0'\delta_0 = \delta_1'\delta_0\), we have \[ \delta_0(\mathrm{P}_{\delta_1}(\mathrm{T},\delta_0(a))) = \delta_1(\mathrm{P}_{\delta_1}(\mathrm{T},\delta_0(a))) \] that is, \(\delta_0(\sigma_i) = \delta_1(\sigma_i)\) for every i. On the other hand, Hamilton-Cayley teaches us that \[ \delta_0(a)^n - \delta_1(\sigma_1)\delta_0(a)^{n-1} + \cdots + (-1)^n\delta_1(\sigma_n) = 0. \] Since \(\delta_1(\sigma_i)\) equals \(\delta_0(\sigma_i)\), we also have \[ \delta_0(a)^n - \delta_0(\sigma_1)\delta_0(a)^{n-1} + \cdots + (-1)^n\delta_0(\sigma_n) = 0, \] whence \[ a^n - \sigma_1a^{n-1} + \cdots + (-1)^n\sigma_n = 0, \] because there exists a homomorphism \(\tau : A_1 \to A_0\) such that \(\tau\delta_0 = \mathrm{id}_{A_0}\), so \(\delta_0\) is injective. It follows that \(A_0\) is integral over B.

b) Now consider two prime ideals x and y of \(A_0\). We shall show that the equality \(x\cap B = y\cap B\) implies the existence of a prime ideal z of \(A_1\) such that \(x = d_0(z)\) and \(y = d_1(z)\):

Indeed, if the assertion were not true, x would be distinct from \(\delta_0^{-1}(t)\) for every prime ideal t of \(A_1\) such that \(\delta_1^{-1}(t) = y\). For such a t we would have \(\delta_0^{-1}(t)\cap B = \delta_1^{-1}(t)\cap B = y\cap B = x\cap B\), from which it would follow, thanks to Cohen-Seidenberg, that x would not be contained in any \(\delta_0^{-1}(t)\) (for there are at most n prime ideals t of \(A_1\) such that \(\delta_1^{-1}(t) = y\)); there would therefore be an \(a\in x\) which would belong to no \(\delta_0^{-1}(t)\); consequently, \(\delta_0(a)\) would belong to none of these t, so that the norm \(\mathrm{N}_{\delta_1}(\delta_0(a))\) would not belong to y (this norm is computed by regarding \(A_1\) as an algebra over \(A_0\) by means of the homomorphism \(\delta_1\); we have \(\mathrm{N}_{\delta_1}(\delta_0(a)) = \sigma_n\) with the notation of a)). But this norm would belong to \(B\cap x = B\cap y\), whence the contradiction.
c) Proof of (i):

Set \(Y = \mathrm{Spec}\ B\) and \(p = \mathrm{Spec}\ i\), where i is the inclusion of B into \(A_0\). We shall first show that \((Y,p)\) is a cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) in the category of all ringed spaces: indeed, it follows from b) that the set underlying \(\mathrm{Spec}\ B\) is obtained from the set underlying \(X_0\) by identifying the points x and y for which there exists \(z\in X_1\) with \(d_1z = y\), \(d_0z = x\). Moreover, since i is integral, \(p = \mathrm{Spec}\ i\) is closed, so that Y carries the quotient topology of that of \(X_0\). Finally, it follows from the choice of B and from the fact that p, \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) are affine, that the canonical sequence of sheaves of rings \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{O}_Y \ar[r] & p_*(\mathscr{O}_{X_0}) \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{p_*(\delta_0)} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{p_*(\delta_1)} & p_*(d_{0*}(\mathscr{O}_{X_1})) = p_*(d_{1*}(\mathscr{O}_{X_1})) } \] is exact, q.e.d.

It remains to show that \((Y,p)\) is also the cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) in the category of schemes (more generally, in that of locally ringed spaces): so let \(q : X_0 \to Z\) be a morphism of schemes such that \(qd_0 = qd_1\). By what precedes, there is one and only one morphism of ringed spaces \(r : Y \to Z\) such that \(q = rp\). We must show that, for every \(y\in Y\), the homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_{r(y)} \to \mathscr{O}_y\) induced by r is local. This follows from the fact that p is surjective, hence that y is of the form \(p(x)\), and from the fact that the homomorphisms \(\mathscr{O}_{q(x)} \to \mathscr{O}_x\) and \(\mathscr{O}_y \to \mathscr{O}_x\) induced by p and q are local.
d) Proof of (ii): Follows from a) and c).
e) Proof of (iii):
Recall that we denote by \(\underline{P}\) the set underlying a scheme P, and by \(\underline{d} : \underline{P} \to \underline{Q}\) the map induced by a morphism \(d : P \to Q\).

One can then rephrase b) by saying that the map \[ \underline{d_0}\boxtimes\underline{d_1} : \underline{X_1} \longrightarrow \underline{X_0}\times_{\underline{Y}}\underline{X_0} \] with components \(\underline{d_0}\) and \(\underline{d_1}\) is surjective; now this map factors as follows \[ \underline{X_1} \xrightarrow{\ \underline{d_0\boxtimes d_1}\ } \underline{X_0\times_Y X_0} \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \underline{X_0}\times_{\underline{Y}}\underline{X_0} \] where q is the canonical map; the image of \(d_0\boxtimes d_1\) therefore contains all the points v of \(X_0\times_Y X_0\) such that \(\{v\} = q^{-1}(q(v))\). This last condition(1) will be satisfied in particular if v is rational over Y, that is, if the residue field \(\kappa(v)\) of v is identified with the residue field \(\kappa(w)\) of the image w of v in Y.

If \(v\in X_0\times_Y X_0\) is not rational over Y, let w still denote the image of v in Y; there then exists a local ring C with radical \(\mathfrak{m}\) and a local flat homomorphism \(f : \mathscr{O}_w \to C\) such that \(C/\mathfrak{m}\) is isomorphic to \(\kappa(v)\) as a \(\kappa(w)\)-algebra. If we set \(Y' = \mathrm{Spec}\ C\) and if \(\pi : Y' \to Y\) is the morphism induced by f, it is clear that the canonical projection of \((X_0\times_Y X_0)\times_Y Y'\) to \(X_0\times_Y X_0\) sends onto v a point \(v'\) of \((X_0\times_Y X_0)\times_Y Y'\) which is rational over \(Y'\). Since \((X_0\times_Y X_0)\times_Y Y'\) is identified with \((X_0\times_Y Y')\times_{Y'}(X_0\times_Y Y')\) and since the arguments made above remain valid after the base change \(\pi : Y' \to Y\), \(v'\) is the image of an element \(u'\in X_1\times_Y Y'\) under the morphism deduced from \(d_0\boxtimes d_1\) by base change. If u is the image of \(u'\) in \(X_1\), we indeed have \(v = (d_0\boxtimes d_1)(u)\), q.e.d.
f) Proof of (iv):

It suffices to show that, for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of B, the homomorphism \((A_0)_{\mathfrak{p}}\otimes_{B_{\mathfrak{p}}}(A_0)_{\mathfrak{p}} \to (A_1)_{\mathfrak{p}}\) with components \(\delta_{0\mathfrak{p}}\) and \(\delta_{1\mathfrak{p}}\) is bijective. In other words, we may assume that B is local. It then follows from b) that \((A_0)_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is semi-local; indeed, if \(\mathfrak{m}\) is a maximal ideal of \((A_0)_{\mathfrak{p}}\), the other maximal ideals are of the form \(\delta_0^{-1}(\mathfrak{n})\), where \(\mathfrak{n}\) (1) N.D.E.: Note the permutation of pages in Lecture Notes 151; the true order is 265-266-268-269-267-270-271.

runs through the prime ideals of \(A_1\) such that \(\delta_1^{-1}(\mathfrak{n}) = \mathfrak{m}\); the assertion thus follows from the fact that there are at most \(n = [A_1 : A_0]\) prime ideals \(\mathfrak{n}\). After possibly performing a faithfully flat base change, we may also assume that the residue field of B is infinite, so that we can use the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2

Let B be a local ring with infinite residue field, A a semi-local ring and \(i : B \to A\) a homomorphism sending the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}\) of B into the radical \(\mathfrak{r}\) of A. Let M be a free A-module of rank n and N a B-submodule of M which generates M as an A-module. Then N contains a basis of M over A.

Indeed, recall that a sequence \(m_1,\ldots,m_n\) of elements of M is an A-basis of M if and only if the canonical images of \(m_1,\ldots,m_n\) in \(M/\mathfrak{r}M\) form a basis of \(M/\mathfrak{r}M\) over \(A/\mathfrak{r}\). One may therefore replace M by \(M/\mathfrak{r}M\), N by \(N/(N\cap\mathfrak{r}M)\), A by \(A/\mathfrak{r}\) and B by \(B/\mathfrak{m}\). In this case the lemma is easy (if A is a product of fields \(K_1\times\cdots\times K_r\), one may identify M with the module \(K_1^n\times\cdots\times K_r^n\); if \(x_j\) is then an element of N whose j-th component in \(K_1^n\times\cdots\times K_r^n\) is nonzero, show that some linear combination x of the \(x_j\) with coefficients in B has all its components nonzero; then replace M by \(M/Ax\) and proceed by induction on n).

We apply the preceding lemma in the following situation: \(B = B\), \(A = A_0\), i is the inclusion of B into \(A_0\), \(M = A_1\) regarded as an \(A_0\)-module by means of the homomorphism \(\delta_1\), \(N = \delta_0(A_0)\): indeed, since \(d_0\boxtimes d_1 : X_1 \to X_0\times_Y X_0\) is a monomorphism, the homomorphism \(A_0\otimes_B A_0 \to A_1\) with components \(\delta_0\) and \(\delta_1\) is surjective; this means precisely that \(\delta_0(A_0)\) generates the \(A_0\)-module \(A_1\).

So let \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) be elements of \(A_0\) such that \(\delta_0(a_1),\ldots,\delta_0(a_n)\) form a basis of \(A_1\) over \(A_0\). If we show that \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) is a basis of \(A_0\) over B, it will follow that the homomorphism \(A_0\otimes_B A_0 \to A_1\) takes the basis \((1\otimes a_i)_{1\leqslant i\leqslant n}\) to the basis \((\delta_0(a_i))_{1\leqslant i\leqslant n}\), hence is bijective. Consequently, if \(\varepsilon : \mathbb{Z}^n \to A_0\) is the homomorphism of abelian groups sending the natural basis of \(\mathbb{Z}^n\) to \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\), it suffices to prove that the map \(B\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}^n \to A_0\) with components i and \(\varepsilon\) is bijective. Now the diagram (0,1,2)* considered at the beginning of this proof induces the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ A_2 & A_1 \ar@<1.1ex>[l]_{\delta_0'} \ar[l]|-{\delta_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[l]^{\delta_2'} \\ A_1\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}^n \ar[u]^{u_2} & A_0\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}^n \ar@<0.6ex>[l]^{\delta_0\otimes\mathbb{Z}^n} \ar@<-0.6ex>[l]_{\delta_1\otimes\mathbb{Z}^n} \ar[u]_{u_1}^{\cong} \\ & B\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}^n \ar[u]_{u_0} \ar[uu]_{i\otimes\mathbb{Z}^n} } \] where \(u_0\), \(u_1\) and \(u_2\) have as components respectively i and \(\varepsilon\), \(\delta_1\) and \(\delta_0\varepsilon\), \(\delta_2'\) and \(\delta_0'\delta_0\varepsilon\). We know that \(u_1\) is an isomorphism. Since the two left-hand squares of (0,1,2)* are cocartesian, \(u_2\) is bijective. Now the two horizontal rows of our diagram are exact; hence \(u_0\) is bijective.

pages.title: 5. Passage to the quotient by a finite flat equivalence prerelation(general case)

blocks.html:
5. Passage to the quotient by a finite flat equivalence prerelation
(general case)

a) Let \(\mathrm{U}^{(n)}\) be the largest open subset of \(X_0\) over which \(d_1\) is finite locally free of rank n. We know that \(X_0\) is the direct sum of the \(\mathrm{U}^{(n)}\). On the other hand, it follows from the two cartesian squares \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar[r]^{d_0'} \ar[d]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar[d]^{d_1} \\ X_1 \ar[r]_{d_0} & X_0 } \qquad\text{and}\qquad \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar[r]^{d_1'} \ar[d]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar[d]^{d_1} \\ X_1 \ar[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] that the inverse images of \(\mathrm{U}^{(n)}\) under \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) both coincide with the largest open subset of \(X_1\) over which \(d_2'\) is locally free of rank n; we thus have \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{U}^{(n)}) = d_1^{-1}(\mathrm{U}^{(n)})\), so that the groupoid \(X_*\) is the direct sum of the groupoids \(X_*^{(n)}\) induced by \(X_*\) on the open and closed subsets \(\mathrm{U}^{(n)}\). It therefore suffices, as is easily seen, to prove Theorem 4.1 for each of the \(X_*^{(n)}\): we are reduced to the case where \(d_1\) is finite locally free of rank n.
b) We are now in a position to prove our theorem in the general case.

By a) we may assume that \(d_1\) is locally free of rank n. So let \((Y,p)\) be a cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) in the category of all ringed spaces. The argument of paragraph 4.c) shows that in order to prove 4.1 (i) it suffices to prove that Y is a scheme and \(p : X_0 \to Y\) a morphism of schemes. The question is therefore local on Y: Let \(y\in Y\) and let \(x\in X_0\) be such that \(p(x) = y\). If x has a saturated affine open neighborhood U, then \(p(U)\) will be an affine open subset of Y by § 4 and \(p|U\) will be a morphism of schemes. It therefore suffices to prove that every \(x\in X_0\) has a saturated affine open neighborhood U. Here is how one proceeds (the proof is taken from SGA 1, VIII cor. 7.6).

\[ d_1(d_0^{-1}(x)) \ \subset\ \mathrm{U} = (\mathrm{V}_f)' \ \subset\ \mathrm{V}_f \ \subset\ \mathrm{V}' \ \subset\ \mathrm{V} \ \subset\ X_0 \] where \(\mathrm{U} = (\mathrm{V}_f)'\) is the largest saturated open subset of \(\mathrm{V}_f\), \(\mathrm{V}_f\) is a special affine open subset of \(\mathrm{V}\), \(\mathrm{V}'\) is the largest saturated open subset of \(\mathrm{V}\), and V is an affine open subset of \(X_0\).

For V one takes an affine open subset containing \(d_1(d_0^{-1}(x))\); if \(\mathrm{F} = \mathrm{X}-\mathrm{V}\), then \(d_1(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{F}))\) is closed, since \(d_1\) is integral, and \(\mathrm{V}' = \mathrm{X}-d_1(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{F}))\) is the largest saturated open subset contained in V. Since \(\mathrm{V}'\) is a neighborhood of the finite set \(d_1(d_0^{-1}(x))\), there exists a section f of the structure sheaf of V which vanishes on \(\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{V}'\) and is such that \(d_1(d_0^{-1}(x))\) is contained in the open subset \(\mathrm{V}_f\) of V consisting of the points where f does not vanish.
We shall see that the largest saturated open subset \((\mathrm{V}_f)'\) of \(\mathrm{V}_f\) is affine, and hence answers the question:

Indeed, let \(\mathrm{Z}(f) = \mathrm{V}'-\mathrm{V}_f\). Then \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{Z}(f))\) is the set of points of \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{V}') = d_1^{-1}(\mathrm{V}')\) where the image \(d_0^*(f)\) of f under the map induced by \(d_0\) vanishes. On the other hand, since \(d_1\) induces a locally free morphism of rank n from \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{V}') = d_1^{-1}(\mathrm{V}')\) onto \(\mathrm{V}'\), \(d_1(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{Z}(f)))\) is the set of points where the norm N of \(d_0^*(f)\) for the morphism \(d_1\) vanishes. It follows that \((\mathrm{V}_f)' = \mathrm{V}'-d_1(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{Z}(f)))\) is the set of points of \(\mathrm{V}_f\) where N does not vanish; consequently, \((\mathrm{V}_f)'\) is affine.
This proves 4.1 (i); the other assertions are clear.

pages.title: 6. Passage to the quotient when a quasi-section exists

blocks.html: 6. Passage to the quotient when a quasi-section exists

We shall now prove a lemma of a technical nature which will serve us in the proof of the two theorems we have in view: Let S be a scheme and \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{d_0'} \ar[r]|-{d_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] a \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid. We shall call a quasi-section of the groupoid \(X_*\) any subscheme U of \(X_0\) satisfying (1) and (2):
(1) The restriction v of \(d_1\) to \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{U})\) is a finite, locally free and surjective morphism from \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{U})\) onto \(X_0\).
(2) Every subset of U consisting of points pairwise equivalent for the equivalence relation defined by the groupoid \(X_*\) (§ 3.e)) is contained in an affine open subset of U.

If U is a quasi-section of \(X_*\), the \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{U}_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{u_0'} \ar[r]|-{u_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{u_2'} & \mathrm{U}_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{u_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{u_1} & \mathrm{U} } \] induced by \(X_*\) and the inclusion of U into \(X_0\) (cf. § 3.a)) satisfies the hypotheses of Theorem 4.1. Indeed, set \(\mathrm{V} = d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{U})\) and let u and v be the morphisms with source V induced respectively by \(d_0\) and \(d_1\): \[ \xymatrix{ X_0 & \mathrm{V} \ar[l]_{v} \ar[r]^{u} & \mathrm{U} } \] By paragraph 3.b), we have a cartesian square \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{U}_1 \ar[r] \ar[d]_{u_1} & \mathrm{V} \ar[d]^{v} \\ \mathrm{U} \ar[r]_{\text{inclusion}} & X_0 } \] so that \(u_1\) is finite locally free by (1). Together with (2), condition (1) thus ensures that the groupoid \(\mathrm{U}_*\) satisfies the hypotheses of Theorem 4.1. In particular \(\mathrm{Coker}(u_0,u_1)\) exists in \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\). Moreover, \(d_0\) admits a section, so that u is a universal effective epimorphism; it follows, by Proposition 3.1, that \(\mathrm{Coker}(u_0,u_1)\) coincides with \(\mathrm{Coker}(v_0,v_1)\) (where \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{V}_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{v_0'} \ar[r]|-{v_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{v_2'} & \mathrm{V}_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{v_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{v_1} & \mathrm{V} } \] denotes the inverse image of \(\mathrm{U}_*\) under the base change \(u : \mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{U}\), that is, equally the inverse image of \(\mathrm{U}_*\) under the base change \(\mathrm{V} \xrightarrow{\ \text{inclusion}\ } X_1 \xrightarrow{\ d_0\ } X_0\)).

Similarly, since v is a universal effective epimorphism, \(\mathrm{Coker}(d_0,d_1)\) coincides with the cokernel of the inverse image of \(X_*\) under the base change \(v : \mathrm{V} \xrightarrow{\ \text{inclusion}\ } X_1 \xrightarrow{\ d_1\ } X_0\). Now this inverse image is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{V}_*\) by paragraph 3.c). We have thus proved the first assertion of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.1

Suppose that the \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid \(X_*\) admits a quasi-section and that \(d_0 : X_1 \to X_0\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact. Then:

  1. (i)

    There exists a cokernel \((Y,p)\) of \((d_0,d_1)\) in \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\); moreover, such a \((Y,p)\) is a cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) in the category of all ringed spaces.

  2. (ii)

    p is surjective; it is open if \(d_0\) is; if S is locally noetherian and \(X_0\) of finite type over S, then Y is of finite type over S.

  3. (iii)

    The morphism \(X_1 \to X_0\times_Y X_0\) with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) is surjective.

  4. (iv)

    If \((d_0,d_1)\) is an equivalence pair, \(X_1 \to X_0\times_Y X_0\) is an isomorphism and p is faithfully flat.
Before proving the second assertion of (i), we shall prove (ii) and (iii):
a) Proof of (ii):

We have just seen that \((Y,p)\) is identified with \(\mathrm{Coker}(v_0,v_1)\) and \(\mathrm{Coker}(u_0,u_1)\). So let q and r be the canonical epimorphisms from U and V to Y: \[ \xymatrix{ & X_0 \ar[drrr]_{p} & & \mathrm{V} \ar[ll]_{v} \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]^{r} & & \mathrm{U} \ar[dl]_{q} \\ & & & & \mathrm{Y} & & } \] Since u and v are surjective, p is surjective if and only if r is, hence if and only if q is. Now q is surjective by Theorem 4.1; whence the surjectivity of p.

Similarly, if \(\mathrm{U}'\) is an arbitrary open subset of U, the saturation of \(\mathrm{U}'\) for the equivalence relation defined by the groupoid \(\mathrm{U}_*\) is none other than \(u_1(u_0^{-1}(\mathrm{U}'))\); it is therefore an open subset of U, from which it follows that the morphism q is open; if \(d_0\) is open, then u is open as well, and so is r; since v is surjective, it follows that p is open.

Now suppose S locally noetherian and \(X_0\) of finite type over S. Let us show that Y is of finite type over S: indeed, since U is quasi-compact over S and q is surjective, Y is quasi-compact over S. Moreover, let \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathrm{R}\) be an affine open subset of S, \(\mathrm{Y}' = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathrm{B}\) an affine open subset of Y projecting into \(\mathrm{S}'\), and \(\mathrm{U}' = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathrm{A}\) the inverse image of \(\mathrm{Y}'\) in U. We must show that B is an R-algebra of finite type; now, by Sections 4 and 5, B is contained in A, which is an R-algebra of finite type; the assertion thus follows from the fact that R is noetherian and A is integral over B.
b) Proof of (iii):

Since the groupoid \(\mathrm{V}_*\) with base V is isomorphic both to the inverse image of \(\mathrm{U}_*\) under the base change u and to the inverse image of \(X_*\) under the base change v, we have a double cartesian square \[ \xymatrix{ X_1 \ar[d]_{d_0\boxtimes d_1} & \mathrm{V}_1 \ar[l] \ar[r] \ar[d]|{v_0\boxtimes v_1} & \mathrm{U}_1 \ar[d]^{u_0\boxtimes u_1} \\ X_0\times_Y X_0 & \mathrm{V}_0\times_Y \mathrm{V}_0 \ar[l]^{v\times v} \ar[r]_{u\times u} & \mathrm{U}_0\times_Y \mathrm{U}_0 } \] Since \(u_0\boxtimes u_1\) is surjective, the same holds for \(v_0\boxtimes v_1\). Since \(v\times v\) is surjective, the same holds for the composite morphisms \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{V}_1 \ar[r]^{v_0\boxtimes v_1} & \mathrm{V}_0\times_Y\mathrm{V}_0 \ar[r]^{v\times v} & X_0\times_Y X_0 } \qquad\text{and}\qquad \xymatrix{ \mathrm{V}_1 \ar[r] & X_1 \ar[r]^{d_0\boxtimes d_1} & X_0\times_Y X_0, } \] hence for \(d_0\boxtimes d_1\).
c) Proof of (i):

It remains to prove that \((Y,p)\) is a cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) in the category of all ringed spaces. We first show that Y is obtained from \(X_0\) by identifying the points x and y for which there exists \(z\in X_1\) with \(d_0(z) = x\) and \(d_1(z) = y\): indeed p is surjective and we have \(pd_0 = pd_1\); furthermore, if \(p(x) = p(y)\), there is a point \(z'\) of \(X_0\times_Y X_0\) whose first projection is x and whose second is y. If z is a point of \(X_1\) such that \((d_0\boxtimes d_1)(z) = z'\), we indeed have \(d_0(z) = x\) and \(d_1(z) = y\).

On the other hand, if W is a saturated open subset of \(X_0\), then \(\mathrm{W}\cap\mathrm{U}\) is a saturated open subset of U; by 4.1, \(q(\mathrm{W}\cap\mathrm{U})\) is an open subset of Y. Since \(q(\mathrm{W}\cap\mathrm{U})\) is none other than \(p(\mathrm{W})\), we see that Y carries a quotient topology of that of \(X_0\).

It remains to prove that the canonical sequence of sheaves of rings \[ \mathscr{O}_Y \longrightarrow p_*(\mathscr{O}_{X_0}) \rightrightarrows p_*d_{0*}(\mathscr{O}_{X_1}) = p_*d_{1*}(\mathscr{O}_{X_1}) \] is exact. So let \(\mathrm{Y}'\) be an open subset of Y and set \(\mathrm{U}' = q^{-1}(\mathrm{Y}')\), \(\mathrm{X}_0' = p^{-1}(\mathrm{Y}')\), etc... It is clear that \(\mathrm{U}'\) is an open subset of U saturated for the equivalence relation defined by the groupoid \(\mathrm{U}_*\). It therefore follows from Theorem 4.1 that \(\mathrm{Y}'\) is the cokernel of the groupoid induced by \(\mathrm{U}_*\) on \(\mathrm{U}'\). Similarly, \(\mathrm{X}_0'\) is an open subset of \(X_0\) saturated for the equivalence relation defined by \(X_*\), and we have \(\mathrm{U}' = \mathrm{X}_0'\cap\mathrm{U}\). Consequently, \(\mathrm{U}'\) is a quasi-section of the groupoid induced by \(X_*\) on \(\mathrm{X}_0'\). What precedes then shows that \(\mathrm{Y}'\) is also the cokernel of this latter groupoid. In particular, for every S-scheme T, we have the exact sequence \[ \xymatrix{ T(\mathrm{Y}') \ar[r]^{\mathrm{T}(p|_{\mathrm{X}_0'})} & T(\mathrm{X}_0') \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{\mathrm{T}(d_0|_{\mathrm{X}_1'})} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{\mathrm{T}(d_1|_{\mathrm{X}_1'})} & T(\mathrm{X}_1'). } \] Now, if T is the "affine line" \(\mathbb{G}_{a,S}\) (I 4.3), this sequence is identified with the sequence: \[ \xymatrix{ \Gamma(\mathrm{Y}',\mathscr{O}_Y) \ar[r] & \Gamma(p^{-1}(\mathrm{Y}'),\mathscr{O}_{X_0}) \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{\delta_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{\delta_1} & \Gamma(d_0^{-1}p^{-1}(\mathrm{Y}'),\mathscr{O}_{X_1}) = \Gamma(d_1^{-1}p^{-1}(\mathrm{Y}'),\mathscr{O}_{X_1}) } \] which is therefore exact for every open subset \(\mathrm{Y}'\).
d) Proof of (iv):

If \((d_0,d_1)\) is an equivalence pair, the same holds for \((u_0,u_1)\). It follows that \(u_0\boxtimes u_1 : \mathrm{U}_1 \to \mathrm{U}_0\times_Y \mathrm{U}_0\) is an isomorphism (Theorem 4.1), hence so is \(v_0\boxtimes v_1\) (compare the cartesian squares of b)); since \(v\times v\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact, \(d_0\boxtimes d_1\) is an isomorphism (SGA 1, VIII 5.4). Moreover, q is faithfully flat, hence so is r, because u is faithfully flat. Since r and v are faithfully flat, the same holds for p.

pages.title: 7. Quotient by a proper flat equivalence prerelation

blocks.html: 7. Quotient by a proper flat equivalence prerelation

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.1

Let S be a locally noetherian scheme and \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{d_0'} \ar[r]|-{d_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] a \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid such that \(d_1\) is proper and flat, \(X_0\) is quasi-projective over S, and the morphism \(d : X_1 \to X_0\times_S X_0\) with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) is quasi-finite. Then:

  1. (i)

    There exists a cokernel \((Y,p)\) of \((d_0,d_1)\) in \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\); moreover, such a \((Y,p)\) is a cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) in the category of all ringed spaces.

  2. (ii)

    p is surjective, open, proper, and Y is of finite type over S.

  3. (iii)

    The morphism \(X_1 \to X_0\times_Y X_0\) with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) is surjective.

  4. (iv)

    If \((d_0,d_1)\) is an equivalence pair, \(X_1 \to X_0\times_Y X_0\) is an isomorphism and p is faithfully flat.

Let \((Y,p)\) be the cokernel of \((d_0,d_1)\) in the category of all ringed spaces. The argument of paragraph 4.c) shows that, in order to prove (i), it suffices to show that Y is a scheme and p a morphism of schemes. Now this question is local on Y; by Lemma 6.1 (i), it therefore suffices to show that every point z of \(X_0\) has a saturated open neighborhood \(\mathrm{U}_z\) such that the groupoid induced on \(\mathrm{U}_z\) by \(X_*\) admits a quasi-section. We may even assume that z is closed in the fiber of z over S (we shall say that z is closed relative to S). The existence of \(\mathrm{U}_z\) then follows from the lemmas below:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.2

Let T be a noetherian affine scheme, X, Y, and Z T-schemes of finite type, with X quasi-projective over T, and \[ \xymatrix{ Y \ar[r]^{u} \ar[d]_{v} & X \ar@{-->}[d] \\ Z \ar@{-->}[r] & T } \] a diagram in \((\mathrm{Sch}/T)\). Let z moreover be a point of Z which is closed relative to T and such that v is flat at the points of \(v^{-1}(z)\). If \(v^{-1}(z)\) is not empty, there exists a closed subscheme F of X such that \(u(u^{-1}(\mathrm{F})\cap v^{-1}(z))\) is finite nonempty and such that the restriction of v to \(u^{-1}(\mathrm{F})\) is flat at the points of \(v^{-1}(z)\).

Let \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathrm{A}\). We may assume X of the form \(\mathrm{Proj}\ \mathrm{S}\), where S is the symmetric algebra of an A-module of finite type E. If \(u(v^{-1}(z))\) is finite, we can choose F equal to X. Otherwise, we denote by \(y_1,\ldots,y_n\) the points of the fiber \(v^{-1}(z)\) associated to the structure sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{v^{-1}(z)}\) of \(v^{-1}(z)\) (the \(y_i\) are thus such that, if \(\mathscr{O}_i\) denotes the local ring of \(v^{-1}(z)\) at the point \(y_i\), the maximal ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_i\) consists of zero divisors). If t is the image of z in T, \(u(v^{-1}(z))\) is an infinite constructible subset of the fiber of t in X. There thus exists a point x closed in this fiber, belonging to \(u(v^{-1}z)\) and distinct from \(u(y_1),\ldots,u(y_n)\). Then \(\mathrm{X}-\overline{\{x\}}\) is an open neighborhood of \(u(y_1),\ldots,u(y_n)\), hence contains an open neighborhood of the form \(\mathrm{D}_+(f)\), where f is a homogeneous element of degree d of S (the notation is that of EGA II, § 2.3). Consequently, the closed subscheme \(\mathrm{X}_1 = \mathrm{V}_+(f)\) defined by f contains x and avoids the points \(u(y_1),\ldots,u(y_n)\). It follows obviously that the inverse image \(\mathrm{Y}_1 = u^{-1}(\mathrm{V}_+(f))\) of this subscheme is distinct from Y and meets \(v^{-1}(z)\).

We shall even show that the restriction \(v_1\) of v to \(\mathrm{Y}_1\) is flat at the points of \(v^{-1}(z)\); if \(u(v_1^{-1}(z))\) is finite, it will thus suffice to choose F equal to \(\mathrm{X}_1\); otherwise, one repeats the argument just developed, replacing Y by \(\mathrm{Y}_1\), v by \(v_1\), and u by the morphism \(u_1\) induced on \(\mathrm{Y}_1\) by u; in this way one obtains a decreasing sequence \(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{X}_1,\ldots\) of closed subschemes of X; since such a sequence terminates, \(u(u^{-1}(\mathrm{X}_n)\cap v^{-1}(z))\) will be finite nonempty for some n and one will choose F equal to \(\mathrm{X}_n\).

It thus remains to show that \(v_1\) is flat at the points of \(v^{-1}(z)\); so let y be a point of \(\mathrm{Y}_1\) above z, \(\mathscr{O}_y\) the local ring of y in Y, \(\overline{\mathscr{O}}_y\) the local ring of y in \(v^{-1}(z)\), \(\mathscr{O}_{v(y)}\) the local ring of \(v(y)\) in Z. If \(g\in \mathrm{S}_1\) is such that \(\mathrm{D}_+(g)\) is a neighborhood of \(u(y)\) in X, let \(\varphi\) be the image of \(f/g^d\) in \(\mathscr{O}_y\) and \(\overline{\varphi}\) the image of \(f/g^d\) in \(\overline{\mathscr{O}}_y\). It then follows from the construction of f that \(\overline{\varphi}\) does not divide 0 in \(\overline{\mathscr{O}}_y\); since \(\mathscr{O}_y\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_z\), \(\varphi\) does not divide 0 in \(\mathscr{O}_y\) and \(\mathscr{O}_y/\mathscr{O}_y\varphi\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_z\) (SGA 1, IV 5.7). Now \(\mathscr{O}_y/\mathscr{O}_y\varphi\) is none other than the local ring of z in \(\mathrm{Y}_1\).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3

We keep the notation and hypotheses of 7.1. Every point z of \(X_0\) which is closed relative to S then has a saturated open neighborhood \(\mathrm{U}_z\) such that the groupoid induced by \(X_*\) on \(\mathrm{U}_z\) admits a quasi-section.

The statement being local on S, we may assume S affine and apply the preceding lemma to the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X_1 \ar[r]^{d_0} \ar[d]_{d_1} & X_0 \ar@{-->}[d] \\ X_0 \ar@{-->}[r] & S } \] in \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\). So let F be a closed subscheme of \(X_0\) such that \(d_0(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{F})\cap d_1^{-1}(z))\) is finite nonempty and such that the restriction of \(d_1\) to \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{F})\) is flat at the points of \(d_1^{-1}(z)\). Denote by \(\mathrm{F}_1\) and \(\mathrm{F}_2\) the inverse images of F under \(d_0\) and \(d_0d_0' = d_0d_1'\), and by \(\widetilde{d}_0, \widetilde{d}_1\ldots\) the morphisms induced by \(d_0, d_1\ldots\) We thus have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{F}_2 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{\widetilde{d}_1'} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{\widetilde{d}_0'} \ar[d]_{\widetilde{d}_2'} & \mathrm{F}_1 \ar[r]^{\widetilde{d}_0} \ar[d]_{\widetilde{d}_1} & \mathrm{F} \\ X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_0} & X_0 & {} } \] in which the two left-hand squares are cartesian and the first row is exact (compare (0,1,2), § 1).

Let us first show that there are only finitely many points of \(\mathrm{F}_1\) above z: indeed, let s be the image of z in S; since \(\widetilde{d}_0(\widetilde{d}_1^{-1}(z))\) is finite, the points of this set are closed in the fiber of s in \(X_0\); there are therefore only finitely many points u of \(X_0\times_S X_0\) whose second projection is z and whose first projection belongs to \(\widetilde{d}_0(\widetilde{d}_1^{-1}(z))\); finally, since \(X_1 \to X_0\times_S X_0\) is quasi-finite, such a point u comes from finitely many points of \(X_1\), whence the assertion. The morphism \(\widetilde{d}_1\) is therefore quasi-finite and flat at the points of \(\mathrm{F}_1\) above z.

Denote by \(\Phi\) the closed subset of \(\mathrm{F}_1\) consisting of the points where \(\widetilde{d}_1\) is not both flat and quasi-finite (SGA 1, IV 6.10 and EGA III, 4.4.10). Since \(d_1\) is proper, \(\widetilde{d}_1(\Phi)\) is closed and does not contain z; consequently, if we set \(\mathrm{W} = \widetilde{d}_1(\mathrm{F}_1) - \widetilde{d}_1(\Phi)\), then \(\widetilde{d}_1\) is flat and quasi-finite, that is, finite and locally free (EGA III, 4.4.2), over W. Since the restriction of \(\widetilde{d}_1\) to \(\widetilde{d}_1^{-1}(\mathrm{W})\) is open, \(\widetilde{d}_1(\mathrm{F}_1)\) is an open neighborhood of z, and W is the largest open subset of \(X_0\) contained in \(\widetilde{d}_1(\mathrm{F}_1)\) over which \(\widetilde{d}_1\) is both flat and quasi-finite.

We shall see in Lemma 7.4 that the inverse images of \(\Phi\) under \(\widetilde{d}_1'\) and \(\widetilde{d}_0'\) are both identified with the set of points of \(\mathrm{F}_2\) where \(\widetilde{d}_2'\) is not both flat and quasi-finite. It follows that \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{W}) = \widetilde{d}_2'(\mathrm{F}_2) - \widetilde{d}_2'(\widetilde{d}_0'^{-1}\Phi)\) coincides with \(d_1^{-1}(\mathrm{W}) = \widetilde{d}_2'(\mathrm{F}_2) - \widetilde{d}_2'(\widetilde{d}_1'^{-1}\Phi)\), that is, W is saturated. Consequently, if we set \(\mathrm{W}_1 = \widetilde{d}_1^{-1}(\mathrm{W})\), the equality \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{W}) = d_1^{-1}(\mathrm{W})\) implies \(\widetilde{d}_2'^{-1}d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{W}) = \widetilde{d}_2'^{-1}d_1^{-1}(\mathrm{W})\), that is, \(\widetilde{d}_0'^{-1}(\mathrm{W}_1) = \widetilde{d}_1'^{-1}(\mathrm{W}_1)\). Since \(\widetilde{d}_0'\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact and the square \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{F}_2 \ar[r]^{\widetilde{d}_1'} \ar[d]_{\widetilde{d}_0'} & \mathrm{F}_1 \ar[d]^{\widetilde{d}_0} \\ \mathrm{F}_1 \ar[r]_{\widetilde{d}_0} & \mathrm{F} } \] is cartesian, it follows that \(\mathrm{W}_1\) is of the form \(\widetilde{d}_0^{-1}(\mathrm{U})\), where U is an open subset of F (SGA 1, VIII 4.4). This open subset U of F is a quasi-section for the groupoid with base W induced by \(X_*\). We may therefore choose \(\mathrm{U}_z\) equal to W.
It thus remains to state Lemma 7.4, whose proof is classical:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.4

Consider a cartesian square of schemes \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{F}_2 \ar[r]^{v} \ar[d]_{d'} & \mathrm{F}_1 \ar[d]^{d} \\ X_1 \ar[r]_{u} & X_0 } \] and let x be a point of \(\mathrm{F}_2\). If u is flat, \(d'\) is flat at x if and only if d is flat at \(v(x)\). If d is locally of finite type, \(d'\) is quasi-finite at x if and only if d is quasi-finite at \(v(x)\).

We have thus proved that there exists a covering of \(X_0\) by saturated open subsets W such that the groupoid \(\mathrm{W}_*\) induced by \(X_*\) on W admits a quasi-section. Modulo Lemma 6.1, this proves Theorem 7.1, except perhaps for the fact that p is proper. For this last point, we take up again the notation of 6.a), replacing the groupoid \(X_*\) by \(\mathrm{W}_*\). Then q is integral by Theorem 4.1, hence proper. Since u is deduced from \(w_0 : \mathrm{W}_1 \to \mathrm{W}_0\) by base change, u is proper; hence r is proper. Since \(\mathrm{W}_0\) is quasi-projective over S, \(\mathrm{W}_0\) is separated and of finite type over Y; moreover, v is surjective; hence p is proper (EGA II, 5.4.3 (ii)).

pages.title: 8. Passage to the quotient by a flat equivalence prerelationnot necessarily proper

blocks.html:
8. Passage to the quotient by a flat equivalence prerelation
not necessarily proper

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.1

Let S be a noetherian scheme and \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{d_0'} \ar[r]|-{d_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] a \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid such that \(d_1\) is flat and of finite type, \(X_0\) is of finite type over S, and the morphism \(X_1 \to X_0\times_S X_0\) with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) is quasi-finite. There then exists a dense saturated open subset \(\mathrm{W}_0\) of \(X_0\) satisfying the following properties:

  1. (i)

    If \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{W}_2 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{w_0'} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{w_1'} & \mathrm{W}_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{w_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{w_1} & \mathrm{W}_0 } \] is the groupoid induced by \(X_*\) on \(\mathrm{W}_0\), then \((w_0,w_1)\) has a cokernel \((\mathrm{V},p)\) in \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\); moreover, \((\mathrm{V},p)\) is a cokernel of \((w_0,w_1)\) in the category of all ringed spaces.

  2. (ii)

    p is surjective, open, and V is of finite type over S.

  3. (iii)

    The morphism \(\mathrm{W}_1 \to \mathrm{W}_0\times_{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{W}_0\) with components \(w_0\) and \(w_1\) is surjective.

  4. (iv)

    If \((d_0,d_1)\) is an equivalence pair, \(\mathrm{W}_1 \to \mathrm{W}_0\times_{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{W}_0\) is an isomorphism and p is faithfully flat.

We shall show that W can be chosen in such a way that the \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid \(\mathrm{W}_*\) induced by \(X_*\) admits a quasi-section (compare § 7). Theorem 8.1 will then follow from Lemma 6.1. Let us admit provisionally that, for every point \(z\in X_0\) closed relative to S (compare § 7), there exists an open subset \(\mathrm{W}_z\) which is saturated, admits a quasi-section, and meets all the irreducible components of \(X_0\) passing through z. Then the exterior \(X_0-\overline{\mathrm{W}_z}\) of \(\mathrm{W}_z\) in \(X_0\) is saturated (for the saturation \(d_1(d_0^{-1}(X_0-\overline{\mathrm{W}_z}))\) of this exterior is open and does not meet \(\mathrm{W}_z\)). If this exterior is not empty, one can choose in it a point \(z'\) closed relative to S and associate to \(z'\) an open subset \(\mathrm{W}_{z'}\) as above; one may moreover assume \(\mathrm{W}_{z'}\) contained in \(X_0-\overline{\mathrm{W}_z}\); then \(\mathrm{W}_z\) and \(\mathrm{W}_{z'}\) are disjoint and the groupoid induced by \(X_*\) on \(\mathrm{W}_z\cup\mathrm{W}_{z'}\) admits a quasi-section... The process must stop because \(X_0\) has only finitely many irreducible components.

It thus remains to construct \(\mathrm{W}_z\): For this we may assume S affine; in this case, let y be a point of \(X_1\) such that \(d_1(y) = z\), X an affine open subset of \(X_0\) containing \(d_0(y)\), Y the inverse image of X in \(X_1\) under \(d_0\), and finally \(u : \mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{X}\) and \(v : \mathrm{Y} \to X_0\) the morphisms induced by \(d_0\) and \(d_1\). Since X is affine, hence quasi-projective, we can apply Lemma 7.2: there is thus a subscheme F of \(X_0\) such that \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{F})\cap d_1^{-1}(z)\) is nonempty, \(d_0(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{F})\cap d_1^{-1}(z))\) is finite, and the restriction of \(d_1\) to \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{F})\) is flat at the points of \(v^{-1}(z)\). This fact allows us to take up again the notation of Lemma 7.3, denoting by \(\mathrm{F}_1\) and \(\mathrm{F}_2\) the inverse images of F in \(X_1\) and \(X_2\), etc... \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{F}_2 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{\widetilde{d}_1'} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{\widetilde{d}_0'} \ar[d]_{\widetilde{d}_2'} & \mathrm{F}_1 \ar[r]^{\widetilde{d}_0} \ar[d]_{\widetilde{d}_1} & \mathrm{F} \\ X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_0} & X_0 & {} } \] One then shows as in 7.3 that \(\widetilde{d}_1\) is quasi-finite at the points of \(\widetilde{d}_1^{-1}(z)\), so that it is natural to consider the open subset \(\mathrm{F}_1'\) of \(\mathrm{F}_1\) consisting of the points where \(\widetilde{d}_1\) is both flat and quasi-finite. By 7.4, the two inverse images of \(\mathrm{F}_1'\) under \(\widetilde{d}_1'\) and \(\widetilde{d}_0'\) consist of the points of \(\mathrm{F}_2\) where \(\widetilde{d}_2'\) is flat and quasi-finite, so that these two inverse images coincide and \(\mathrm{F}_1'\) is of the form \(\widetilde{d}_0^{-1}(\mathrm{F}')\), where \(\mathrm{F}'\) is an open subset of F (SGA 1, VIII 4.4). After replacing F by \(\mathrm{F}'\), we may therefore assume that \(\widetilde{d}_1\) is quasi-finite and flat.

In this case, we denote by \(\mathrm{W}_z\) the largest open subset of \(\widetilde{d}_1(\mathrm{F}_1)\) over which \(\widetilde{d}_1\) is finite and flat. This open subset \(\mathrm{W}_z\) contains the generic points of the irreducible components of \(X_0\) passing through z. It then follows from SGA 1, VIII, 6.5 and 5.7, that \(d_0^{-1}(\mathrm{W}_z)\) and \(d_1^{-1}(\mathrm{W}_z)\) both coincide with the largest open subset of \(\widetilde{d}_2'(\mathrm{F}_2)\) over which \(\widetilde{d}_2'\) is finite and flat. Consequently one sees as in 7.3 that the two inverse images of \(\widetilde{d}_1^{-1}(\mathrm{W}_z)\) under \(\widetilde{d}_0'\) and \(\widetilde{d}_1'\) coincide, hence that \(\widetilde{d}_1^{-1}(\mathrm{W}_z)\) is of the form \(\widetilde{d}_0^{-1}(\mathrm{U})\) where U is an open subset of F which is a quasi-section for the groupoid induced by \(X_*\) on \(\mathrm{W}_z\).

pages.title: 9. Elimination of the noetherian hypotheses in Theorem 7.1

blocks.html: 9. Elimination of the noetherian hypotheses in Theorem 7.1

a) Let us take up again the notation and hypotheses of Lemma 6.1 and let \(\pi : \mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) be an arbitrary base change. Denote by \(f' : \mathrm{X}' \to \mathrm{Y}'\) the morphism of \(\mathrm{S}'\)-schemes deduced by the extension \(\pi\) of the base from a morphism of S-schemes \(f : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\). With this convention, \(p' : \mathrm{X}_0' \to \mathrm{Y}'\) is surjective, as is the morphism \(\mathrm{X}_1' \to \mathrm{X}_0'\times_{\mathrm{Y}'} \mathrm{X}_0'\) with components \(d_0'\) and \(d_1'\). The set underlying \(\mathrm{Y}'\) is thus identified with the quotient of the set underlying \(\mathrm{X}_0'\) by the equivalence relation defined in \(\mathrm{X}_0'\) by the \(\mathrm{S}'\)-groupoid \(\mathrm{X}_*'\). Moreover, \(q' : \mathrm{U}' \to \mathrm{Y}'\) is integral surjective, so that the topology of \(\mathrm{Y}'\) is the quotient topology of that of \(\mathrm{U}'\), hence also of that of \(\mathrm{X}_0'\) (compare the proof in § 6.c)).

On the other hand, it is clear that \(\mathrm{U}'\) is a quasi-section of the \(\mathrm{S}'\)-groupoid \(\mathrm{X}_*'\), to which we can therefore apply Lemma 6.1. In particular, \(\mathrm{X}_*'\) has a cokernel \((\mathrm{Y}_1,p_1)\) and the topological space underlying \(\mathrm{Y}_1\) is obtained from the topological space underlying \(\mathrm{X}_0'\) by identifying the points equivalent for the relation defined by \(\mathrm{X}_*'\). It follows that the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{Y}_1 \to \mathrm{Y}'\) is a homeomorphism; I even claim that \(\mathrm{Y}_1 \to \mathrm{Y}'\) is a universal homeomorphism: indeed, if \(\mathrm{S}''\) is over \(\mathrm{S}'\), let \(\mathrm{Y}_2\) be the cokernel of \((d_0\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{S}'',\ d_1\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{S}'')\). By what precedes, applied to the base changes \(\mathrm{S}''\to\mathrm{S}'\) and \(\mathrm{S}''\to\mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm{Y}_2\to\mathrm{Y}_1\times_{\mathrm{S}'}\mathrm{S}''\) and \(\mathrm{Y}_2\to \mathrm{Y}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{S}'' \simeq \mathrm{Y}'\times_{\mathrm{S}'}\mathrm{S}''\) are homeomorphisms, so that the same holds for \(\mathrm{Y}_1\times_{\mathrm{S}'}\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{Y}'\times_{\mathrm{S}'}\mathrm{S}''\).
b) Analogous remarks obviously apply to the case where the groupoid \(X_*\) admits quasi-sections "locally" (compare the proof of Theorem 7.1).

Let for example S be an arbitrary scheme and \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1.1ex>[r]^{d_0'} \ar[r]|-{d_1'} \ar@<-1.1ex>[r]_{d_2'} & X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^{d_0} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_{d_1} & X_0 } \] a \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid on which we make the following hypotheses: \(X_0\) is of finite presentation and quasi-projective over S; \(d_1\) is of finite presentation, proper and flat; the morphism \(X_1 \to X_0\times_S X_0\) with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) is quasi-finite. Then every point x of \(X_0\) has an open neighborhood W which is saturated and such that the groupoid induced by \(X_*\) in W admits a quasi-section:

Indeed, the question is local on S, so that we may assume S affine. There then exists a ring A of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\), a morphism \(\mathrm{S}\to \mathrm{T} = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathrm{A}\) and a \((\mathrm{Sch}/T)\)-groupoid \(\mathrm{Z}_*\) such that \(X_*\) is identified with \(\mathrm{Z}_*\times_{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{S}\) (EGA IV, 8); moreover, we may assume that \(\mathrm{Z}_*\) satisfies the conditions of Theorem 7.1 (EGA IV, 8). Consequently \(\mathrm{Z}_*\) admits quasi-sections "locally" and we can apply the remarks of a).

c) It follows from a) and b) that assertions (i), (iii) and (iv) of Theorem 7.1 remain true under the hypotheses of b). When moreover \((d_0,d_1)\) is an equivalence pair, one sees that Y is of finite presentation over S by applying the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.1

Consider morphisms of schemes \[ \xymatrix{ X_0 \ar[r]^{p} & Y \ar[r]^{q} & S } \] such that \(qp\) is of finite type (resp. of finite presentation) and p is faithfully flat of finite presentation. Then q is of finite type (resp. of finite presentation)(*).

Since p is surjective and \(qp\) quasi-compact, q is quasi-compact. We may therefore assume S, Y and \(X_0\) affine with rings A, B, C: We have \(\mathrm{B} = \varinjlim \mathrm{B}_i\), where the \(\mathrm{B}_i\) run through the subalgebras of finite type of B. Since C is of finite presentation over B, there exist an index \(i_0\), a \(\mathrm{B}_{i_0}\)-algebra of finite presentation \(\mathrm{C}_{i_0}\) and an isomorphism \(\mathrm{C} \simeq \mathrm{C}_{i_0}\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_{i_0}} \mathrm{B}\); if we set \(\mathrm{C}_i = \mathrm{C}_{i_0}\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_{i_0}} \mathrm{B}_i\) for \(i\geqslant i_0\), we thus have \(\mathrm{C} \simeq \mathrm{C}_i\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_i} \mathrm{B}\). \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{B} \ar[r] & \mathrm{C} \\ \mathrm{B}_i \ar[u] \ar[r] & \mathrm{C}_i \ar[u] \\ \mathrm{A} \ar[u] & {} } \] Since C is faithfully flat over B, one deduces from EGA IV 11 the existence of an \(i_1\geqslant i_0\) such that \(\mathrm{C}_{i_1}\) is faithfully flat over \(\mathrm{B}_{i_1}\); consequently \(\mathrm{C}_i\) is faithfully flat over \(\mathrm{B}_i\) for \(i\geqslant i_1\). For \(i\geqslant i_1\), the canonical map \(\mathrm{C}_i \to \mathrm{C}\) is then injective, being deduced from \(\mathrm{B}_i \to \mathrm{B}\) by faithfully flat extension of the base. If C is of finite type over A, it follows that the map \(\mathrm{C}_i \to \mathrm{C}\), hence also the map \(\mathrm{B}_i \to \mathrm{B}\), are bijective for i large enough.

Now suppose C of finite presentation over A: by what precedes, B is of finite type over A, hence of the form \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}/\mathrm{I}\) where \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}\) is a polynomial algebra over A in finitely many indeterminates, and I an ideal of \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}\). Then I is the union of its subideals of finite type \(\mathrm{I}_\alpha\); whence the equality \(\mathrm{B} = \varinjlim \mathrm{B}_\alpha\) with \(\mathrm{B}_\alpha = \overline{\mathrm{B}}/\mathrm{I}_\alpha\). Proceeding as above, one chooses an index \(\alpha_0\) and a \(\mathrm{B}_{\alpha_0}\)-algebra of finite presentation \(\mathrm{C}_{\alpha_0}\) such that there exists an isomorphism \(\mathrm{C} \simeq \mathrm{C}_{\alpha_0}\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_{\alpha_0}} \mathrm{B}\). For \(\alpha\geqslant\alpha_0\), one again sets \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha = \mathrm{C}_{\alpha_0}\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_{\alpha_0}} \mathrm{B}_\alpha\), so that we have \(\mathrm{C} \simeq \mathrm{C}_\alpha\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_\alpha} \mathrm{B}\) for \(\alpha\geqslant\alpha_0\). Again by EGA IV 11, one concludes as above that \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha\) is faithfully flat over \(\mathrm{B}_\alpha\) for \(\alpha\) large enough. In this case, the kernel of the map \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha \to \mathrm{C}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha \to \mathrm{C}_\beta\) for \(\beta\geqslant\alpha\)) is identified with \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_\alpha} (\mathrm{I}/\mathrm{I}_\alpha)\) (resp. with \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_\alpha} (\mathrm{I}_\beta/\mathrm{I}_\alpha)\)). Since \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha\) and C are of finite presentation over A and \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha \to \mathrm{C}\) is surjective, \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_\alpha} (\mathrm{I}/\mathrm{I}_\alpha)\) is an ideal of finite type and is the union of the ideals \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_\alpha} (\mathrm{I}_\beta/\mathrm{I}_\alpha)\). We therefore have \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_\alpha} (\mathrm{I}_\beta/\mathrm{I}_\alpha) = \mathrm{C}_\alpha\otimes_{\mathrm{B}_\alpha} (\mathrm{I}/\mathrm{I}_\alpha)\) for \(\beta\) large enough, whence also \(\mathrm{I}_\beta = \mathrm{I}\) (for \(\mathrm{C}_\alpha\) is faithfully flat over \(\mathrm{B}_\alpha\)); hence B is of finite presentation over A.
(*) Cf. EGA IV4, 17.7.5 for a more general result.
